experiment: H_ISL — IRDME Symbolic Language: hub-star ISL compression preserves structural identity
description: First empirical test of H_ISL. Core question: can a graph be expressed as symbolic hub-star generator rules (ISL), and does the ISL-reconstructed graph preserve IRDME structural identity (hub ranks, FPL sign)? Test graph: linux_kernel (30 nodes, 114 edges, 3 layers). ISL representation: hub-star rules for top 33% nodes (10 hubs) as sources — one rule per (hub, layer) pair. Residual edges (41/114, from non-hub sources) are dropped. Reconstruction uses only hub-star rules. Structural identity is tested by comparing the original full graph to the ISL-only reconstruction.

hypotheses.statement: ISL hub-star rules achieve compression: n_rules < n_hub_edges (22 rules cover 73 edges). Compression ratio = n_rules / n_hub_edges < 0.5.
hypotheses.note: Compression ratio is structurally deterministic from hub selection — this confirms the ISL rule format compresses. Pre-confirmed at 0.301.

hypotheses.statement: ISL reconstruction preserves hub rank ordering: Spearman r between total_degree ranks in original vs ISL-reconstructed graph > 0.80.
hypotheses.note: Key structural identity test. High-d1 hubs (vfs, irq, process) have fully preserved outgoing degree in reconstruction. High-d2 hubs (mm, scheduler) receive connections primarily from other hubs which are included.

hypotheses.statement: FPL sign is preserved: r(d1, d2) has the same sign in the original graph and the ISL-reconstructed graph.
hypotheses.note: Original linux_kernel r(d1,d2) = -0.342. ISL drops non-hub edges but hub structure dominates the correlation direction. If hub topology determines FPL sign, sign is preserved.

hypotheses.statement: Top-3 hubs by total_degree are identical in original and ISL-reconstructed graph (at least 2/3 match).
hypotheses.note: Original top-3: mm(17), scheduler(15), block(15). All three are in the hub set and appear as targets of multiple hub sources — their incoming degree is partially preserved.

analysis_plan.step1: Load linux_kernel.json, compute degrees d1/d2 per node per layer, rank all nodes by total_degree
analysis_plan.step2: Extract hub-star rules: for each hub in top-10, for each layer, list all its targets. Output: list of (hub, layer, [targets])
analysis_plan.step3: Reconstruct ISL graph: expand hub-star rules back to explicit edges. Discard residual edges.
analysis_plan.step4: Compute d1/d2/total_degree for all nodes in reconstructed graph
analysis_plan.step5: Measure h1: count rules, compute ratio
analysis_plan.step6: Measure h2: Spearman r between original and reconstructed total_degree rank vectors
analysis_plan.step7: Measure h3: compute r(d1,d2) for reconstructed graph, compare sign to original (-0.342)
analysis_plan.step8: Measure h4: identify top-3 in reconstructed, count overlap with original top-3

notes: This is a prototype ISL test using the simplest possible rule type (hub-star). H_ISL_v2 would test abstract rules (shared-target merging) and chain rules. The scientific question is whether structural identity is preserved under hub-dominated symbolic compression — not whether ISL is production-ready.
